Name: spectral_latency_optimization
Title: Spectral Latency Optimization: A Training-Free Framework for Low-Latency Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks can be leveraged to develop a training-free framework for low-latency inference, which dynamically allocates computational resources based on real-time activation geometry and spectral decay rates.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works in training-free machine learning methods, such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation. However, our approach differs in that it focuses on latency optimization and uses spectral stability bounds to guide the allocation of computational resources. Unlike previous methods, our framework does not rely on static profiling or costly calibration, and instead uses real-time diagnostics to adapt to dynamic input distributions.
Abstract: We propose a training-free framework for low-latency inference, which leverages spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks to dynamically allocate computational resources. Our approach uses real-time activation geometry and spectral decay rates to identify the most critical components of the input data and allocate resources accordingly. We evaluate our framework on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks and demonstrate its ability to achieve a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier while enforcing strict latency budgets.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on single-GPU setups across diverse benchmarks, including WILDS and ICL benchmarks.
Compare the performance of our framework with state-of-the-art training-free methods, such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation.
Investigate the impact of different spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks on the performance of our framework.
Evaluate the robustness of our framework to distributional shifts and noisy contexts.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed framework relies on real-time diagnostics, which may introduce additional computational overhead.
The framework may not perform well on datasets with complex or dynamic distributions.
The choice of spectral stability bounds and PAC-Bayes frameworks may significantly impact the performance of the framework.
